Lecture 1: Python Introduction (13/01/2026) ¶

Live Class ¶

  • Module Lead: Ryan Cory-Wright (r.cory-wright@imperial.ac.uk)
    • Assistant Professor of Analytics and Operations at Imperial Business School since 2023
    • Prev: Postdoc at IBM, PhD in Operations Research at MIT
    • Research: Optimization, Machine Learning, Statistics, Renewable Energy, AI
    • Coding: Julia (native), Python, MATLAB, C++, Java, etc.
  • Tutorial Leader 1: Jay DesLauriers (j.deslauriers@imperial.ac.uk)
    • Senior Teaching Fellow in the Early Career Researcher Institute
    • AI Futurist in Education for the Business School
    • Research Fellow at the University of Westminster in cloud and swarm computing
    • Work with Early Career Institute, providing training to PhD, post-docs
  • Tutorial Leader 2: Marius Mickunas (m.mickunas@imperial.ac.uk)
    • Tutor at Imperial Business School, Guest lecturer at the London Business School and ESMT Berlin
    • PhD in Immunology from King's College London
    • Data Analytics with Python/R, Statistics, Data Visualization, Optimization, Decision and Risk Analysis
  • Data is digitised information
    • Data structures describe ways to organise data
    • Algorithms describe how we process data:
      • Step-by-step instructions
      • Take input data and produce output data
  • Computers interpret and execute programs
    • Computers are good at performing well-defined calculations and remembering the results
      • We also need good algorithm design
    • Computers have a harder time solving less well-defined problems (understanding humor or deciding which art is good)
  • The course focuses on understanding the first category: what constitutes a good algorithm
  • Moore's Law - computing chips roughly double in power every 2-3 years
  • Two lessons from Madoff ponzi scheme in choosing different options for investing:
    • The future might not look like the past
    • Data may not tell the whole story; you also need context
      • Where to put extra armor on British RAF planes based on mapping of bullet holes
        • Put where there aren't a lot of holes
  • Exercise: write out a sequence of 20 coin tosses that look random.
    • The longest consecutive sequence of "H" is $$log_2(20)-1=3.33$$
  • Philosophy: programmers are made, not born
    • One-half lecture, one-half practical coding
    • Mix of optional readings/supplementary material and lectures focusing on the most important ideas
  • Course objective: introduction to computational problem-solving through the design of algorithms and data structures
    • Learn to build algorithms by moving from an ambiguous problem statement to formulating a computational solution method
    • Provide a supportive environment for you to learn how to implement them in Python
  • Don't use ChatGPT!
  • What is Python?
    • Python is a free object-oriented programming language that is widely used in industry
    • Beginner-friendly: easier to debug than languages like C or C++
    • More popular than similarly user-friendly alternatives like Julia or Java
    • Many useful data science and machine learning libraries developed natively in Python

Python Exercises ¶

  • Readings:
    • Guttag Chapters 1, 2.0-2.5
    • OR
    • Sweigart, Al. Automate the Boring Stuff with Python.
      • Chapter 1 – Basics https://automatetheboringstuff.com/chapter1/
      • Chapter 2 – Control flow (until for loop) https://automatetheboringstuff.com/chapter2/
  • Syntax: structure of language, what constructs and statements are valid
    • "Dog moose cow." doesn't make sense
    • In Python, we needed to be precise with syntax
  • Semantics is the meaning of the statements
    • My dog flew the kitchen sink to Hawaii." doesn't make sense
  • Codespaces are a cloud-based environment for coding, provided by a version-control platform called GitHub
    • GitHub allows us to keep different versions of the code, useful for adding new features or fixing bugs
  • ok system that test our understanding of Python and code runs on command line
    • Command line: text-based interface for interacting with a computer

Question 0: OK-check ¶

  1. Which of the following is often used by Data Scientists?
    1. [CORRECT] All three are.
    2. R
    3. Python
    4. SQL
  2. When was Python first released?
    1. 2016
    2. 1972
    3. [CORRECT] 1991
    4. 1867

Expressions and calculations ¶

  • python gives access to the Python interpreter via a console
    • We can then type in commands, which the Python interpreter will evaluate-
    • quit() takes you back to the command line
  • Script: code files that saves code for fture use
  • Integrated Development Environment (IDE): software application that combines essential tools for writing, testing, and debugging code into a single interface
  • Computer programs consist of expressions, which tell the computer how to combine pieces of data
In [12]:
10 - 1
Out[12]:
9
In [13]:
2 * 3
Out[13]:
6
In [14]:
15 / 5 # notice that the division result is always a decimal number
Out[14]:
3.0
In [15]:
24 / 3
Out[15]:
8.0
  • Python calculations follow standard precedence order of arithmetic, where multiplication and division are evaluated before addition and subtraction
  • We can use parentheses to alter the order of precedence, as follows
In [16]:
3 - 8 * 5
Out[16]:
-37
In [17]:
(3 - 8) * 5
Out[17]:
-25
Operator Name Description
a + b Addition Sum of a and b
a - b Subtraction Difference of a and b
a * b Multiplication Product of a and b
a / b Division Quotient of a and b
a // b Floor division Quotient of a and b, removing fractional parts
a % b Modulus Remainder after division of a by b
a ** b Exponentiation a to the power of b
In [18]:
2 ** 3
Out[18]:
8
In [19]:
10 // 8
Out[19]:
1
In [20]:
10 % 8
Out[20]:
2
In [20]:
5 / 0 # produces an error

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ZeroDivisionError                         Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[20], line 1
----> 1 5 / 0

ZeroDivisionError: division by zero

Question 1: Calculations ¶

  1. 3 - 8
    1. -5
  2. 5*7
    1. 35
  3. 1/2
    1. 0.5
  4. 60 / 3
    1. 20.0
  5. 3**3
    1. 27
  6. 8//5
    1. 1
  7. 8 % 5
    1. 3

Variables and assignment ¶

  • Variable: name we assign to a value
    • We use an equals sign = (variable = expression)
    • We take the values stored in the variables width and length and save the result of their multiplication to a new variable area
In [22]:
width = 7
length = 3
area = width * length
area
Out[22]:
21
  • Variables are called variables because their value can change
  • Assigning a value to a variable that already exists does not create a new variable, but replaces the existing value of the variable
In [23]:
temp = 20
temp = 50
temp
Out[23]:
50
In [22]:
temp = (50 - 30) * 2
temp
Out[22]:
40
  • We can also change variable by referring to itself
In [24]:
x = 5
y = 3
x = x + y
x
Out[24]:
8
  • Referencing a variable that hasn't been assigned gives an error message
  • Variable names have to only use letters, numbers, and underscores
  • Names cannot have spaces in them, and there are some keywords that are reserved for Python's use
In [24]:
height

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
NameError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[24], line 1
----> 1 height

NameError: name 'height' is not defined
In [25]:
123var = 3 # cannot start name with a number

  Cell In[25], line 1
    123var = 3 # cannot start name with a number
      ^
SyntaxError: invalid decimal literal
In [26]:
this doesnt = 4 # cannot have a space in name - use underscores instead

  Cell In[26], line 1
    this doesnt = 4 # cannot have a space in name - use underscores instead
         ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax
In [27]:
if = 5 # if is a reserved Python keyword
# VS Code will alert you if you try to use one of them

  Cell In[27], line 1
    if = 5 # if is a reserved Python keyword
       ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax

Displaying output ¶

  • The print function displays the value of the expression we place insider the following parenthesis
In [28]:
width = 7
print(2*width)

14
  • Strings: text data
    • A collection of characters within quotation marks, where the characters can be anything: numbers, letters, or other characters
    • Define strings using either single or double quotes

Question 2: Variables ¶

  1. a = 5
    b = 6
    c = a * b
    c
    1. 30
  2. z = 1
    b = 5
    z = z + b
    z
    1. 6
  3. a = 5
    b = 6
    c = a * b
    a = 100
    c
    1. 30
  4. a = 5
    b = 6
    temp = a
    a = b
    b = temp
    print(a)
    print(b)
    1. (line 1) 6
    2. (line 2) 5

Handling text ¶

In [25]:
print(3 + 3)
print('3 + 3')

6
3 + 3
  • First is result of calculation
    • Second is a string of characters because we entered it within quotes
  • Strings can be defined by either single or double quotes
In [29]:
x = "a string"
x
Out[29]:
'a string'
In [30]:
print(x)

a string
  • When typing in the variable name, it retrieves its value, a string with quotation marks
  • Printing it strips away the quotation marks and displays only the content of the string
  • Strings can be concatenated using +
    • *n duplicates the string n times
In [26]:
x = 'Franz '
y = 'Kafka'
z = x + y
print(z)

Franz Kafka
In [27]:
print(x*3)

Franz Franz Franz
  • In a string, to disply single quotes, single quotes need to have backslash before them (\' is an escape character)
    • We can also use double quotes instead
  • \n inserts a line break
In [28]:
print('Kafka\'s \nbook')
print("Kafka's book")

Kafka's 
book
Kafka's book
  • Triple quotes create multiline strings
In [33]:
text = """
Franz Kafka
Metamorphosis
"""
print(text)

Franz Kafka
Metamorphosis

Question 3: Strings ¶

  1. x = 'Franz'
    y = 'Kafka'
    z = x + y
    print(z)
    1. FranzKafka
  2. x = 'Franz'
    y = 'Kafka'
    c = ' '
    z = x + c + y
    print(z)
    1. Franz Kafka
  3. print('Two'1 + 'Three'2)
    1. TwoThreeThree
  4. phrase = 'It wasn't me'
    print(phrase)
    1. It wasn't me
  5. phrase = 'It wasn't me'
    1. Error

Objects, types, and built-in functions ¶

  • Objects: strings and numbers and anything else we can manipulate
    • What we can do with the object depends on the type of the object
    • type() function figures out the type of an object
In [35]:
type('5')
Out[35]:
str
In [36]:
type(5)
Out[36]:
int
In [37]:
type(5.0)
Out[37]:
float
  • Python distinguishes between integer-valued numbers and decimals, which are called floating-point numbers or floats
In [30]:
3 + '4'

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[30], line 1
----> 1 3 + '4'

TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'int' and 'str'
  • This throws a TypeError as they are different types
  • int() function converts a string into an integer
  • float() function converts a string into a float
  • str() converts numbers into strings
In [34]:
x = 3
y = '4'
y_int = int(y)
type(y_int)
Out[34]:
int
In [35]:
x + y_int
Out[35]:
7
In [39]:
int(3.5)
Out[39]:
3
  • The above is an expression, but of a different kind
    • A call expression applies a Python function to an argument
    • Function: maps an input argument into an output value
      • Above, the function int is applied to the argument 3.5 and the output value of the evaluated expression is 3
  • The following are some common functions:
In [36]:
max(3, 5) # output: maximum value among input arguments
Out[36]:
5
In [37]:
min(3, 5) # output: maximum value among input arguments
Out[37]:
3
In [38]:
abs(-4) # output: absolute value of input argument
Out[38]:
4
In [39]:
round(5.324, 1) # output: rounds the first argument to the number of decimals specified by second argument
Out[39]:
5.3
In [40]:
len('hello') # output: length of input string
Out[40]:
5
  • Each of these functions can be used to assign the output value to a variable:
In [41]:
y = max(3, 4)
y
Out[41]:
4
  • The output of the print function is always the special value None, which represents "nothing" as it displays the argument only
In [42]:
print(3)

3
In [43]:
three = print(3) # displays the argument; output is none
print(three)

3
None
  • Because Python is a dynamically-typed language, you don't need to define variables always by first specifying their types unlike static typing

Question 4: Objects and types ¶

  1. type('23')
    1. str
  2. type(42)
    1. int
  3. number = '3'
    number = int(number)
    number + 2
    1. 5
  4. number = '3'
    type(number * 4)
    1. str
  5. number = '3'
    print(number + 2)
    1. Error
  6. c = 5.1
    s = str(c)
    print('The number is ' + s)
    1. The number is 5.1
  7. number = '3'
    number = float(number * 4)
    number
    1. 3333.0
  8. number = '3'
    number = float(number + '5')
    number
    1. 35.0
  9. int(2.5) + 2.5
    1. 4.5
  10. max(3, 5.0)
    1. 5.0
  11. type(max(3, 1.0))
    1. int
  12. type(max(3, 10.0))
    1. float
  13. round(3.8)
    1. 4
  14. type(round(4.53))
    1. int
  15. type(round(4.53, 1))
    1. float

Writing programs in the VS Code editor ¶

  • When writing longer programs, it is useful to type them in a text editor before running the program
    • By creating a Python file with .py suffix, we can run a code and only display information we want to see using print() instead of intermediary commands and calculations
In [42]:
first_name = 'Franz'
last_name = 'Kafka'
book_name = 'The Trial'
book_year = 1925
text = first_name + ' ' + last_name + ' published ' + book_name + ' in ' + str(book_year)
print(text)

Franz Kafka published The Trial in 1925

Comparisons and conditional statements ¶

  • Boolean expressions give result that is always either True (1) or False (0)
In [43]:
2 > 1
Out[43]:
True
In [44]:
1 > 2
Out[44]:
False
In [45]:
1 == 0
Out[45]:
False
In [46]:
1 != 0
Out[46]:
True
In [47]:
type(1>2)
Out[47]:
bool
In [48]:
True == 1
Out[48]:
True
In [44]:
False == 0
Out[44]:
True
Operation Description
a == b a equal to b
a != b a not equal to b
a < b a less than b
a > b a greater than b
a <= b a less than or equal to b
a >= b a greater than or equal to b
  • We can reverse a comparison by using the keyword not
In [45]:
a = 5*5
b = 25
a == b
Out[45]:
True
In [46]:
not a == b
Out[46]:
False
  • Boolean expressions can also be combined using the operators and and or
  • Logical and evaluates to True only when both Booleans are True
  • Logical or evaluates to True when at least one of the Booleans is True
In [50]:
True and False
Out[50]:
False
In [51]:
True or False
Out[51]:
True

Question 5: Comparisons ¶

  1. print(2 > 2)
    1. False
  2. print(2 >= 2)
    1. True
  3. print(not (5 > 2))
    1. False
  4. x = 5
    y = 4
    print(x > y and x < 8)
    1. True

Conditional Statements ¶

  • Boolean expressions allow us to make choices using conditional statements
    • Conditional if statements provide one way of doing this
      • The statement consists of a condition and a body block
      • If the condition is True, then the statements of the body block are executed
        • Otherwise, they are skipped
  • Syntax notes
    • The line with the if statement ends with a colon
    • The following code is indented by four spaces (equivalent to Tab in VS Code)
    • Code Block: group of indented linesd
    • Decision Structure: program that makes decisions based on data, branching in different directions
      • if statement is a decision structure
In [52]:
rainfall = 2
if rainfall > 0.5:
    print('It will likely rain ' + str(rainfall) + ' millimeters.')
    print('Bring an umbrella!')

It will likely rain 2 millimeters.
Bring an umbrella!
In [53]:
rainfall = 0
if rainfall > 0.5:
    print('It will likely rain ' + str(rainfall) + ' millimeters.')
    print('Bring an umbrella!')
print('Done with checking the weather.')

Done with checking the weather.
  • If we want to print a different message when the if statement condition isn't met, then we use an else statement (only executes if the if condition is False)
In [54]:
rainfall = 0
if rainfall > 0.5:
    print('It will likely rain ' + str(rainfall) + ' millimeters.')
    print('Bring an umbrella!')
else:
    print('Expected rainfall is below 0.5 millimeters today.')
    print('Enjoy your day!')
print('Done with checking the weather.')

Expected rainfall is below 0.5 millimeters today.
Enjoy your day!
Done with checking the weather.
  • We can also go through several cases of a condition using elif, short for "else if"
In [55]:
temp = 13
if temp >= 30:
    print('Too hot!')
elif temp <= 15:
    print('Too cold!')
else:
    print('Perfect weather!')
    print('Have a great day!')
print('Done with the temperature.')

Too cold!
Done with the temperature.
  • Nested statements have condition(s) inside another condition
    • The second if statement below only gets considered if the first if statement condition is True
In [56]:
temp = 33
humidity = 90
if temp >= 30:
    print('Too hot!')
    if humidity > 80:
        print('I\'m out of here!')

Too hot!
I'm out of here!

Question 6: Conditionals ¶

  1. x = 5
    if x > 5:
         print('Hey!')
    1. Hey!
  2. dish = 'broccoli'
    if dish == 'potatoes':
         print('My favorite food!')
    else:
         print('Yuck.')
    1. Yuck.
  3. temp = 0
    if temp >= 30:
         print('Too hot!')
    elif temp <= 0:
         print('Too cold!')
    else:
         print('Perfect!')
    1. Too cold!
  4. temp = 18
    humidity = 90
    if temp >= 30:
         print('Too hot!')
         if humidity > 80:
             print('I'm out of here!')
    1. Nothing
  5. temp = 40
    humidity = 90
    if temp >= 30:
         print('Too hot!')
         if humidity > 80 and humidity < 100:
             print('Hot and kind of humid!')
         elif humidity < 80:
             print('Hot but not too humid!')
         else:
             print('I'm out of here!')
    1. (line 1) Too hot!
    2. (line 2) Hot and kind of humid!

Iteration using while loops ¶

  • Iteration mechanisms (loops) allow us to repeatedly execute statements
  • while loops executes the "body" statements continuously while the condition is True until the condition turns False
    • We often increment a variable that is part of the condition of the while loop to limit the number of iterations the body is run for
In [47]:
print(1**2)
print(2**2)
print(3**2)
print(4**2)
print(5**2)

1
4
9
16
25
In [57]:
i = 1 # initialization
while i <= 5: # condition
    print(i**2)
    i = i + 1  # increment

1
4
9
16
25
  • Two indented lines of code are repeated as long as the condition i <= 5 is satisfied
  • Three key components of while loops: initialization, condition, and incrementing
  • In VS Code, the key combination Ctrl + C quits the current running code

Question 7: Loops ¶

  1. n = 2
    while n >= 0:
         n = n - 1
         print(n)
    1. (line 1) 1
    2. (line 2) 0
    3. (line 3) -1
  2. n = 1
    while n >= 0:
         n = n + 1
    print(n)
    1. Infinite loop
  3. y = 2
    while y != 0:
         y = y // 2
         print(y)
    1. (line 1) 1
    2. (line 2) 0

Syntax roundup ¶

  • Python syntax
    • End of the line terminates a statement
    • Parenthesis are used for calling Python functions, such as print(), or grouping, as in the calculations above
    • The hash sign # denotes a comment
    • Whitespace within the line has no effect on the execution
    • Whitespace in the beginning of the line matters a lot
      • Extra spaces can cause IntentationError, which is when the indent is wrong
      • Indentation is used to determine which parts of the code are part of blocks such as if statements
      • The colon sign : is used to denote the beginning of such an indented block

Error roundup ¶

  • Python errors
    • SyntaxError: an error in Python syntax, for example in my name = 'John'
    • TypeError: combining wrong type of data, for example in 1/'2'
    • IndentationError: when the code is not properly aligned
    • NameError: when a reference is made to a local variable in a function or method, but no value has been bound to that variable
    • ValueError: function receiving a wrong type of value, for example in int('a')

Optional Exercises ¶

Question 8: What would Python display (optional) ¶

  1. 8 // 2 * 3 % 5
    1. 2
  2. 2 ** 4 // 3 + 2 ** (4 // 3)
    1. 7
  3. price_2015 = 400000
    price_2016 = 500000
    price_pct_change = (price_2016 - price_2015) / price_2015 * 100
    print(price_pct_change)
    1. 25.0
  4. print('I have \n one \n two \n three \n books')
    1. (line 1) I have
    2. (line 2)   one  
    3. (line 3)   two  
    4. (line 4)   three  
    5. (line 5)   books
  5. a = '9.95'
    b = float(a)
    type(b)
    1. float
  6. str(int(float(str(1.5))) + 2.5)
    1. '3.5'
  7. float(str(4.2)) + float(int(float(str(4.2))))
    1. 8.2
  8. print(1 and not 0 == 5)
    1. True
  9. print(0 >= 0 or 0 and True)
    1. True
  10. print(0 >= 0 and 0 and True)
    1. 0
  11. print(5 * 4 == 20, 5 >= 5, not 5 != 4)
    1. True True False
  12. first_name = 'Franz'
    print(first_name == 'Franz', type(first_name) == str)
    1. True True
  13. ssum, i = 0, 0
    while i < 4:
         ssum = ssum + i
         i = i + 1
    print(ssum)
    1. 6
  14. ssum, i = 0, 0
    while i < 4:
         i = i + 1
         ssum = ssum + i
    print(ssum)
    1. 10
  15. ssum, i = 0, 0
    while i < 4:
         if i % 2 == 0:
             i = i + 2
         else:
             i = i + 1
         ssum = ssum + i
    print(ssum)
    1. 6
  16. ssum, i = 0, 0
    while i < 84:
         if i % 2 == 0:
             i = i + 6
         else:
             i = i + 1
         ssum = ssum + i * (i % 2)
    print(ssum)
    1. 0
  17. ssum, i = 'start:', 0
    while i < 5:
         i = i + 1
         ssum = ssum + ' ' + 'i' * i
    print(ssum)
    1. start: i ii iii iiii iiiii
  18. x, sr, cnt = 24, 1, 0
    while abs(sr * sr - x) > 0.1:
         sr = (sr + x / sr) / 2.0
         cnt += 1
    print(cnt)
    1. 5
  • Comma in print() conjugates with space in between
  • or returns one of the operands (first "truthy" value)
  • and returns one of the operands (first "falsy" value or last value if all are "truthy")

Question 9: Voting Condition (optional) ¶

In [58]:
# Define a variable called age and assign it the value 19 (or a different integer)
# Write a condition that prints out the string 'You're old enough to vote.' if age is greater than or equal to 18.
# Otherwise, the condition prints out 'Sorry, you still have to wait out x years before you can vote.',
# where x is the difference between 18 and age
# You'll need to use at least an if-structure, a type conversion, and the print function.
age = 16
if age >= 18:
    print("You're old enough to vote.")
else:
    x = 18 - age
    print("Sorry, you still have to wait out " + str(x) + " years before you can vote.")

Sorry, you still have to wait out 2 years before you can vote.

Question 10: Loop Practice (optional) ¶

  • Here's a piece of code that asks the user for a number and then uses a while loop to do a countdown to zero.
In [60]:
number = input("Please enter a number.")
number = int(number) # the input is by default a string, so we need to convert it to integer
while number > 0:
    print(number, "!")
    number = number - 1
print("BOOM!")

4 !
3 !
2 !
1 !
BOOM!

(a) Edit the loop such that it only prints every other number.

In [55]:
number = input("Please enter a number.")
number = int(number) # the input is by default a string, so we need to convert it to integer
while number > 0:
    print(number, "!")
    number = number - 2
print("BOOM!")

6 !
4 !
2 !
BOOM!

(b) Next, make a loop that instead starts from 1 and prints out BOOM! after we reach the input number. To do so, we have to initialize a variable i before the loop, to increment the number to be printed. Copy the following code into the editor and complete it such that it works correctly.

In [56]:
number = input("Please enter a number.")
number = int(number) # the input is by default a string, so we need to convert it to integer
i = 1
while i <= number:
    print(i, "!")
    i = i + 1
print("BOOM!")

1 !
2 !
3 !
4 !
5 !
6 !
BOOM!

(c) Finally, write a loop (or loops) that work as follows, first asking for three different inputs and then using them to print a rectangle of the name in the specified shape

In [58]:
name = input("Please enter your name.")
width = int(input("Enter integer width."))
height = int(input("Enter integer height."))
i = 0

while i < height:
    print((name + "! ") * width)
    i = i + 1

Sarah! Sarah! Sarah! 
Sarah! Sarah! Sarah! 
Sarah! Sarah! Sarah! 
Sarah! Sarah! Sarah! 
Sarah! Sarah! Sarah!

Tutorial 1 (16/01/2026) ¶

  • Oasis Reunion Tour Case Study
    • After years of speculation, Oasis announced their 2025 reunion tour. Over 10 million fans tried to buy tickets for the available 1.4 million seats. To maximise revenue in the face of this overwhelming demand, Ticketmaster implemented a dynamic pricing model.
    • The company set the initial ticket price at £148.50. For the first million tickets sold, the price increased by £2 after every 100,000 tickets sold. The sale of that millionth ticket, though, triggered a £50 price increase instead. Each subsequent 100,000 tickets sold also incurred a £50 price increase, instead of the £2 price increase.
    • As prices climbed, public criticism mounted. The government is now considering introducing a 30% cap above the initial price for any ticket sold through dynamic pricing models. Ticketmaster needs to calculate how this potential price cap would have affected their revenue.
  • Summary
    • 10 million fans try to buy ticket for 1.4 million available seats
    • Ticketmaster implement dynamic pricing model
    • Initial ticket price at £148.50
      • For the first million tickets sold, the price increased by £2 after every 100,000 tickets sold
      • The sale of millionth ticket triggers a £50 price increase
      • Each subsequent 100,000 tickets sold also incurs £50 price increase
    • Government considering 30% cap above initial price for any ticket sold
      • Calculate how this potential price cap affect their revenue
In [59]:
# Initialise variables
tickets_sold = 0
revenue = 0
ticket_price = 148.50
cap = ticket_price * 1.3

# use a loop to repeat
while tickets_sold < 1_400_000: # underscore is ignored by Python but useful for readability

    print("current price:", ticket_price)

    # sell in blocks that correspond to price changes
    tickets_sold += 100_000 # equivalent to tickets_sold = tickets_sold + 100_000
    print(tickets_sold, "tickets have been sold")

    # only increment the revenue by the size of the block
    revenue += 100_000 * ticket_price
    
    # one conditional to handle the different price jumps
    if tickets_sold < 1_000_000:
        ticket_price += 2
    else:
        ticket_price += 50

    # another conditional to handle the cap
    if ticket_price > cap:
        ticket_price = cap

print("Total revenue:", revenue)

current price: 148.5
100000 tickets have been sold
current price: 150.5
200000 tickets have been sold
current price: 152.5
300000 tickets have been sold
current price: 154.5
400000 tickets have been sold
current price: 156.5
500000 tickets have been sold
current price: 158.5
600000 tickets have been sold
current price: 160.5
700000 tickets have been sold
current price: 162.5
800000 tickets have been sold
current price: 164.5
900000 tickets have been sold
current price: 166.5
1000000 tickets have been sold
current price: 193.05
1100000 tickets have been sold
current price: 193.05
1200000 tickets have been sold
current price: 193.05
1300000 tickets have been sold
current price: 193.05
1400000 tickets have been sold
Total revenue: 234720000.0

Lecture 2: Functions and Algorithms (20/01/2026) ¶

Live Class ¶

  • Goals:
    • Explain what a function is and why we use them
    • Write a simple function with a return statement
    • Describe an algorithm as step-by-step instructions for turning input into output
    • Implement an algorithm in Python and sanity-check it on small examples
In [60]:
x = 5
6 = y
print(x)
print(y)

  Cell In[60], line 2
    6 = y
    ^
SyntaxError: cannot assign to literal here. Maybe you meant '==' instead of '='?
In [61]:
a = 2
b = a
a = 5
print(a)
print(b)

5
2
In [63]:
x = 5
if x >= 0:
    print(1)
elif x < 20:
    print(2)
else:
    print(3)
print(4)

1
4
In [64]:
x = 3
while x > 0:
    print(x)
    x = x-1

3
2
1
In [65]:
x = 3
while x > 0:
    x = x - 1
print(x)

0
In [70]:
abs(-3)
Out[70]:
3
In [66]:
max(5, 4, 10)
Out[66]:
10
In [67]:
max(abs(-5), min(3, 9))
Out[67]:
5
  • We call the function, specifying the arguments within parentheses
  • Function is named group of statements to perform a specific task
    • Input data -> function -> output data
    • A function may have multiple parameters separated by commas
      • May return multiple values separated by commas
  • Why functions?
    • Abstraction: user does not need to know what happens inside
    • Make code easily re-usable and modular
    • Changing code becomes easier as we don't have to copy same code in many places
  • When copying code from somewhere else, make it into a function
In [73]:
def abs_value(a):
    if a < 0:
        return -a
    else:
        return a

abs_value(-3)
Out[73]:
3
  • Reliable pattern for writing functions
    • Step 1: what are the inputs/outputs?
    • Step 2: write down the function
In [76]:
def is_even(n: int) -> bool:
    """Return True iff n is an even integer."""
    return (n % 2) == 0
  • Step 3: test on small instances immediately
In [78]:
assert is_even(0) is True # returns AssertionError if condition doesn't match
In [79]:
assert is_even(1) is False
In [80]:
assert is_even(10) is True
  • Goal: calculate a square root ($y \times y = x$)
    • Algorithm (Heron of Alexandria, first century AD):
      • Make a guess, for example $x/2$
      • Repeat three times:
        • Divide the original number $x$ by the guess to get a ratio
        • Find the average of the guess and the ratio
        • Use this average as the next guess
In [81]:
def square_root(x):
    guess = x/2
    eps = 0.01
    while abs(guess*guess-x) >= eps:
        guess = (guess + x/guess)/2
    return guess

square_root(20)
Out[81]:
4.472137791286727
In [82]:
print(square_root(20)**2)

20.000016424254923
  • Taylor Series Expansion of a Function $$f(x)=f(a)+\sum_{k=1}^\infty \frac{f^{(k)}(a)(x-a)^k}{k!}$$ $$\sqrt{x} \approx h_0 + \frac{x-h_0^2}{2h_0} = \frac{\frac{x}{h_0}+h_0}{2}$$
  • Heron's formula is a second-degree expansion of the Taylor Series approximation for the square root function $\sqrt{x}$ about $x = h_0^2$
  • Problem setting: given an integer $n$, decide whether $n$ is prime
  • Algorithm (Brute-Force):
    • Check whether $n$ is exactly divisible by any integer between 2 and $\lfloor \sqrt{n} \rfloor$ (inclusive)
  • Improvements:
    • Only check whether $n$ is divisible by prime number, so we can accelerate method using recursion
    • If $n > 2$ and even, then it's not prime, so we can restrict to odd numbers and search between 3 and $\lfloor \sqrt{n} \rfloor$
    • Many, many more refinements
In [76]:
import math
def is_prime(n):
    if isinstance(n, int):
        if n <= 1:
            return False
        elif n == 2:
            return True
        elif n % 2 == 0:
            return False
        else:
            for i in range(3, int(math.sqrt(n)) + 1, 2):
                if n % i == 0:
                    return False
            return True
    else:
        return 'Please ensure n is an Int'
    
is_prime(31)
Out[76]:
True
  • Problem setting: find local minimum of continuously differentiable univariate function $f(x)$
    • We want $f'(x_{new})=0$ and some other conditions (e.g. $f''(x_{new}) > 0$, sometimes $f''(x_{new} \ge 0$) for $x_{new}$ to minimize $f$
    • Taylor series expansion of $f'$ about $x_{new}$ $$f'(x_{new}) = f'(x) + f''(x_{new}-x) + \dots \approx f'(x) + f''(x)(x_{new}-x)$$
    • If $x$ is our current guess and we want to find an $x_0$ where $f'(x_{new})=0$, we should set $$f'(x) + f''(x)(x_{new} - x) = 0$$ $$x_{new} - x = -\frac{f'(x)}{f''(x)}$$ $$x_{new}=x-\frac{f'(x)}{f''(x)}$$
    • Repeating this until we converge is called Newton's method
In [81]:
def newton_method(f_prime, f_doubleprime, x0, tol=1e-6, max_iter=100):
    
    x_min = x0
    iterations = 0
    
    while True:
        f_dash = f_prime(x_min)
        f_doubledash = f_doubleprime(x_min)
    
        if abs(f_dash) < tol:
            break
            
        x_min = x_min - f_dash / f_doubledash
    
        iterations += 1
    
        if iterations >= max_iter:
            break
    
    return x_min, iterations

def f_prime(x):
    return 3*x**2 - 3

def f_doubleprime(x):
    return 6*x

newton_method(f_prime, f_doubleprime, x0=2)
Out[81]:
(1.0000000464611474, 4)
  • Traceback is Python telling you: (1) what failed, (2) where, and (3) why
    • Start at the bottom line: it names the error (e.g. NameError, TypeError)
    • Go one step up to find the failing line
    • Ask question to find what caused the answer
  • If stuck:
    • Check for typos/spelling/type of each variable
    • Read each line and ask yourself what it does, then run it in the common prompt and check whether it does what you think it does
    • Read the docs
    • Read error messages carefully, and Google if you don't understand what they mean
    • Try to see whether your code solves a particularly simple version of the problem and then go from there
    • For a given input, write out what each step of the answer should be by hand and see whether the command prompt matches what you wrote
    • Try to print variables at intermediate points in the code, and see if what they say is what you think they should say
  • Homework 1 (out of 2)
    • Two parts: a written part (5%) of your overall grade and a coding part (5%) of your overall grade. The homework is worth 10% of your grade overall.
    • Grade for coding part is based on correctness (whether your code works for different inputs), efficiency (how fast it is) and best practice with respect to commenting etc.
    • Individual programming exercises - the College takes plagiarism very seriously. We refer all plagiarism instances to the program team, who are quite strict
    • Do not share your solution to any HW assignment with anyone.
    • Some questions require things we will learn next session

Python Exercises ¶

  • Readings:
    • Guttag: Chapters 3.1, 4
    • OR
    • Sweigart, Al. Automate the Boring Stuff with Python.
      • Chapter 3 – Functions https://automatetheboringstuff.com/chapter3/
  • Optional Readings:
    • Ford, Paul. What is code?
      • https://www.bloomberg.com/graphics/2015-paul-ford-what-is-code/

Question 1: Recap ¶

  1. print(4 * 3)
    1. 12
  2. x = 2
    y = 3
    x = (y + 1)*x
    print(x)
    1. 8
  3. temp = 30
    print('Temp: ' + temp)
    1. Error
  4. x = 8
    x < 5
    x > 0 and x < 8
    1. False
    2. False
  5. name = 'Franz'
    if name == 'Franz':
         print('Hello ' + name)
    1. Hello Franz
  6. y = 7
    while y > 0:
         y = y // 2
         print(y)
    1. 3
      1
      0
  7. y = 2
    while y != 0:
         y = y // 2
         print(y)
    1. 1
      0

Functions ¶

  • Abstraction of functions is powerful
    • We can use the functions to build more interesting calculations without worrying about the details behind the calculations
  • Function Call: function_name(arguments)
    • Argument: expression within the parentheses of the function call
      • Several argument expressions separated by commas are evaluated one at a time, from left to right
      • Resulting values are passed to the function
      • Function is then executed and produces an output value
In [11]:
temp_morning = 5
temp_afternoon = 10
abs(temp_morning - temp_afternoon)
Out[11]:
5
  • Function calls can also be nested
In [75]:
min(abs(-1), max(3, 0))
Out[75]:
1
  • If you don't know exactly what a function does, you can type help(function_name)

Defining a function ¶

  • Python's built-in functions, such as print, type, and abs, are useful but limited
  • We can also define new functions
    • First line is the function header, consisting of the keyword def, whcih tells Python we are defining a function
      • Followed by function name miles_to_km and a comma-separated list of parameters (here, just miles) within parentheses, and finally a colon
    • After the colon, we have a sequence of statements that form the function body
      • Statements executed whenever we call the function
      • By convention, this is exactly four spaces, which in VS Code is a Tab key
    • Keyword return tells Python that this is the value we want to produce as the output of the function
      • With the return, the function call finishes, and Python literally returns to the place in our program where the function was called
      • Here the resulting value gets assigned to the variable x
In [84]:
def miles_to_km(miles):
    return miles * 1.609

x = miles_to_km(30)
x
Out[84]:
48.269999999999996
  • In general, a function is called as follows:
def function_name(parameters):
    statement
    statement
function_name(arguments)

Printing and returning ¶

  • For functions that simply displays some text in console, we can write the function without any value returned (the special value None, which indicates an "empty" or "null" value)
In [83]:
def print_compliment(name):
    print('Great job, ' + name + '!')
    return

print_compliment('John')

Great job, John!
In [86]:
x = print_compliment('John')
print(x)

Great job, John!
None
  • Python allows us to omit the return statement altogether, but still acts as if return has value None
  • The below code works exactly like the code above:
In [88]:
def print_compliment(name):
    print('Great job, ' + name + '!')

print_compliment('John')

Great job, John!
  • If we want to use the text outside the function, we neeed to return the text
In [87]:
def print_compliment(name):
    return 'Great job, ' + name + '!'

text = print_compliment('John')
print(text)
print(text)

Great job, John!
Great job, John!
  • Function also doesn't have to have parameters
In [91]:
def print_compliment_donald():
    print('Well done, Donald!')
    print('Great job!')

print_compliment_donald()

Well done, Donald!
Great job!

A common error ¶

  • Just like a variable, if a function isn't defined before calling it, we get a NameError
In [92]:
print_personal_compliment('Donald')

def print_personal_compliment(name):
    print('Well done, ' + name + '!') # insert the name into the phrase

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
NameError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[92], line 1
----> 1 print_personal_compliment('Donald')
      3 def print_personal_compliment(name):
      4     print('Well done, ' + name + '!')

NameError: name 'print_personal_compliment' is not defined

Local variables ¶

  • We are calling a local variable first (also a parameter of the function) that is created within the function
    • These local variables only exist when the function is being executed, and disappear when we return from the function call
    • When we try to use a local variable from outside the function call, we therefore get a NameError
  • Part of program where we can use a variable is called its scope
    • Scope of variable defined within a function is the function call
In [94]:
def max_value(first, b):
    if first > b:
        return first
    else: 
        return b

c = max_value(3, 5)
print(c)
print(first)

5

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
NameError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[94], line 9
      7 c = max_value(3, 5)
      8 print(c)
----> 9 print(first)

NameError: name 'first' is not defined
  • At the global level, in the Python interpreter, there is a "frame" (or "table") of names keeping track of all names defined at that level and their assigned values
  • When a function is called, a new frame is created to keep track of all names within that function call
    • This could result in many such tables, as a function could call another function that could call another function, etc
  • When a function call is completed, its frame disappears
    • Some values may be returned to the frame from which the function was called

Question 2: Functions ¶

  1. def mult(x,y):
    product  = x * y
    return product
mult(5,2)
    1. 10
  2. def some_printing(text):
    if text == 'hey':
        print(text)
    else:
        print('hello')
some_printing('hi')
    1. hello
  3. def return_one():
    return 1
return_one()
    1. 1
  4. return_one() + 1
    1. 2
  5. return_one + 1
    1. Error
  6. type(return_one)
    1. function
  7. type(return_one())
    1. int
  8. def print_one():
    print(1)
    return 1
z = print_one()
print(z)
    1. (line 1) 1
    2. (line 2) 1
  9. def print_one():
    print(1)
    return 1
print(print_one())
    1. (line 1) 1
    2. (line 2) 1
  10. def print_one():
    print(1)
    return None
z = print_one()
print(z)
    1. (line 1) 1
    2. (line 2) None
  11. def print_one():
    print(1)
z = print_one()
print(z)
    1. (line 1) 1
    2. (line 2) None
  12. def go(n):
    while n > 0:
        print(n)
        n = n // 2
go(4)
    1. (line 1) 4
    2. (line 2) 2
    3. (line 3) 1

Question 3: Sum of squares ¶

  • Function sum_of_squares containss first a string explaining what the function does and giving some examples of its output for different inputs
    • This is called a documentation string or docstring for short
  • When working on a function, you can test your implementation in the Python console by clicking on any part of the defined function and then pressing shift+enter to load the function in the terminal
    • Then use the terminal to call your function with differnt inputs to test it
In [89]:
def sum_of_squares(x, y):
    """
    Returns the sum of squares of x and y

    Examples:
    >>> sum_of_squares(1, 2)
    5
    >>> sum_of_squares(100, 3)
    10009
    >>> sum_of_squares(-1, 0)
    1
    >>> x = sum_of_squares(2, 3)
    >>> x + 1
    14
    """
    # DON'T CHANGE ANYTHING ABOVE
    # YOUR CODE BELOW THIS
    return (x**2 + y**2) # REPLACE THIS LINE WITH YOUR CODE
In [90]:
sum_of_squares(1, 2)
Out[90]:
5
In [91]:
sum_of_squares(100, 3)
Out[91]:
10009
In [92]:
sum_of_squares(-1, 0)
Out[92]:
1
In [93]:
x = sum_of_squares(2, 3)
x + 1
Out[93]:
14

Question 4: Print grade ¶

In [94]:
def print_grade(mark, grade_high, grade_low):
    """
    Prints out 'distinction', 'pass', or 'fail' depending on mark

    If mark is at least grade_high, prints 'distinction'
    Else if mark is at least grade_low, prints 'pass'
    Else prints 'fail'
    
    Examples:
    >>> grade_high = 70 
    >>> grade_low = 50
    >>> print_grade(20, grade_high, grade_low)
    fail
    >>> print_grade(61, 70, 50)
    pass
    >>> print_grade(90, 80, 60)
    distinction
    """
    # DON'T CHANGE ANYTHING ABOVE
    # YOUR CODE BELOW THIS
    if mark >= grade_high:
        print("distinction")
    elif mark >= grade_low:
        print("pass")
    else:
        print("fail")
In [95]:
grade_high = 70 
grade_low = 50
print_grade(20, grade_high, grade_low)

fail
In [96]:
print_grade(61, 70, 50)

pass
In [97]:
print_grade(90, 80, 60)

distinction

Question 5: Weeks ¶

  • Function takes as argument two different integers in the range 1-365
    • The numbers represent days in the same year
    • Function calculates the differe between the two days in weeks and returns that value
In [98]:
def weeks(first, second):
    """
    Calculates the number of full weeks between the two inputs. Either input may be higher.
    
    Parameters:
    first, second: integers in the range 1-365, first != second.

    Returns:
        full weeks between the two numbers

    Example use:
    >>> weeks(20, 1)
    2
    >>> weeks(1, 21)
    2
    >>> weeks(1, 22)
    3
    >>> weeks(346, 281)
    9
    """
    # DON'T CHANGE ANYTHING ABOVE
    # YOUR CODE HERE
    return (abs(first - second) // 7)
In [109]:
weeks(20, 1)
Out[109]:
2
In [110]:
weeks(1, 21)
Out[110]:
2
In [111]:
weeks(1, 22)
Out[111]:
3
In [112]:
weeks(346, 281)
Out[112]:
9

Function recap ¶

  • Functions are very useful because they:
    • Allow us to write a set of commands once and execute them whenever needed
      • This means that we can save a lot of time and effort when writing larger programs
    • Create useful abstractions
      • Once the function works, we don't care about the code inside the function anymore - we just use it by calling it
      • We can write small parts of programs and use them to create larger programs
    • Make it easier to change our code, by being modular
      • Whenever we want to change our code, we only need to do it in one place only, and it will work similarly every time the function is called
      • Without functions, we would have to make the same changes in multiple places in our programs, making us more vulnerable to bugs and mistakes

Designing functions ¶

  • When starting to write a function, we find it useful to start by stating out what we want the function to do
  • We'd like to give the function a name that gives a clear indication of this purpose, and a comment which states this more clearly
  • So we start out writing:
In [99]:
def fahrenheit_to_celsius(fahrenheit):
    """
    Converts temperature from Fahrenheit to Celsius to two decimals.
    """
    pass

help(fahrenheit_to_celsius)

Help on function fahrenheit_to_celsius in module __main__:

fahrenheit_to_celsius(fahrenheit)
    Converts temperature from Fahrenheit to Celsius to two decimals.
  • Here, pass is a keyword used as a placeholder for code we haven't written yet
  • The help function prints out the docstring of a function
  • The next step is determining what the function should do, i.e. what its output should be for different inputs
  • If we look up the conversion formula, we can use it to calculate what the result should be, for example
>>> fahrenheit_to_celsius(10)
-12.22
>>> fahrenheit_to_celsius(-20)
-28.89
  • It's useful to add these code examples to the docstring to make the output clear to users
  • We also often want to clarify the types of input and output of the function, as follows:
In [100]:
def fahrenheit_to_celsius(fahrenheit):
    """
    Converts temperature from Fahrenheit to Celsius to two decimals.
    
    Parameters:
    fahrenheit: temperature in fahrenheit
    
    Returns:
    temperature in celsius
    
    Example use:
    >>> fahrenheit_to_celsius(10)
    -12.22
    >>> fahrenheit_to_celsius(-20)
    -28.89
    """
    pass
  • Now, after specifying what the function should do, we are ready to write the program to give us the desired result
In [101]:
def fahrenheit_to_celsius(fahrenheit):
    """
    Converts temperature from Fahrenheit to Celsius to two decimals.
    
    Parameters:
    fahrenheit: temperature in fahrenheit
    
    Returns:
    temperature in celsius
    
    Example use:
    >>> fahrenheit_to_celsius(10)
    -12.22
    >>> fahrenheit_to_celsius(-20)
    -28.89
    """
    celsius = (fahrenheit - 32)*5/9
    return round(celsius, 2)

fahrenheit_to_celsius(-20)
Out[101]:
-28.89

Optional Exercises ¶

Question 6: Middle of three (optional) ¶

In [122]:
def middle_of_three(a, b, c):
    """
    Returns the middle one of three numbers a,b,c
    Examples:
    >>> middle_of_three(5, 3, 4)
    4
    >>> middle_of_three(1, 1, 2)
    1
    """
    # DON'T CHANGE ANYTHING ABOVE
    # YOUR CODE BELOW

    return (a+b+c - max(a,b,c) - min(a,b,c))
In [123]:
middle_of_three(5, 3, 4)
Out[123]:
4
In [124]:
middle_of_three(1, 1, 2)
Out[124]:
1

Question 7: Sum up to (optional) ¶

In [115]:
def sum_up_to(n):
    """
    Returns the sum of integers from 1 to n
    
    Examples:
    >>> sum_up_to(1)
    1
    >>> sum_up_to(5)
    15
    """
    # DON'T CHANGE ANYTHING ABOVE
    # YOUR CODE BELOW
    
    return int(n*(n+1)/2)
In [116]:
sum_up_to(1)
Out[116]:
1
In [117]:
sum_up_to(5)
Out[117]:
15

Question 8: Faster than Heron? (optional) ¶

  • We will update Heron's algorithm so that it counts the number of iterations it does and returns the count along with the square root value
  • Next, we'll compare Heron's approach with another classic algorithm called bisection search
    • We know that the square root of a number $x$ is somewhere between $0$ and $x$
    • We make a guess: the square root is the midpoint of this range
    • Like with Heron, if the guess is close enough, we stop
    • If the guess is too low, we know that the square root is not in the bottom half so we can only search the top half
    • Otherwise, we similarly discard the top half
    • We then pick the midpoint of the updated range as a new guess
    • We repeat this until we're close enough
  • The algorithm is thus as follows:
    • Start with a guess $g$ as average of search range $low=0$ and $high=\max\{1.0,x\}$
    • If $g\cdot g$ is close to $x$, stop and return $g$ as the answer
    • Otherwise, if $g \cdot g < x$, update search range: $low = g$
    • Otherwise, if $g \cdot g \ge x$, update search range: $high = g$
    • Make new guess as average of updated search range
    • Repeat process using new guess until close enough
  • We can translate this as a while loop:
    • Start with a guess $g$ as average of search range $low=0$ and $high=\max\{1.0,x\}$
    • While $g \cdot g$ is not "close" to $x$:
      • If $g \cdot g < x$, update search range: $low = g$,
      • If $g \cdot g \ge x$, update search range: $high = g$
      • Make new guess as average of updated search range $\{low,high\}$
    • When the while loop finishes, return the final guess
In [125]:
def square_root_heron(x, epsilon=0.01):
    """
    Find square root using Heron's algorithm

    Parameters:
    x: integer or float
    epsilon: desired precision,
        default value epsilon = 0.01 if not specified

    Returns:
    the square root value, rounded to two decimals
    the number of iterations of the algorithm run

    Example use:
    >>> y, c = square_root_heron(20)
    >>> print(y, c)
    4.47 4
    """
    # DON'T CHANGE ANYTHING ABOVE
    # UPDATE CODE BELOW
    
    n = 0
    guess = x/2 # Make initial guess
    # Loop until squared value of guess is close to x
    while abs(guess*guess - x) >= epsilon:
        guess = (guess + x/guess)/2 # Update guess using Heron's formula
        n += 1
    return round(guess, 2), n # replace the dots with the final number of iterations
In [126]:
square_root_heron(20)
Out[126]:
(4.47, 4)
In [120]:
def square_root_bisection(x, epsilon=0.01):
    """
    Find square root using bisection search

    Parameters:
    x: integer or float
    epsilon: desired precision,
        default value epsilon = 0.01 if not specified

    Returns:
    the square root value, rounded to two decimals
    the number of iterations of the algorithm run

    Example use:
    >>> y, c = square_root_bisection(20)
    >>> print(y, c)
    4.47 9
    """
    # DON'T CHANGE ANYTHING ABOVE
    # UPDATE CODE BELOW
    
    n = 0
    low = 0.0
    high = max(1.0, x) # Why are we doing this? What would happen for x=0.5?
    guess = (low + high)/2 # First guess at midpoint of low and high
    while abs(guess*guess - x) >= epsilon:
        if guess*guess < x:
            low = (low + high)/2 # update low
        else:
            high = (low + high)/2 # update high
        guess = (low + high)/2 # new guess at midpoint of low and high
        n += 1
    return round(guess, 2), n
In [121]:
square_root_bisection(20)
Out[121]:
(4.47, 9)

Question 9: Guess the number game (optional) ¶

  • Write a Guess the number game
    • Computer draws a random (integer) number and prompts the user to guess the number
    • If the guess is correct, the program ends
    • Otherwis prints out "Too low" or "Too high" and prompts the user again
In [122]:
import random

def guess_the_number(min_value, max_value):
    x = random.randint(min_value, max_value)
    name = input('What is your name?')
    guess = input('Guess a number between ' + str(min_value) + ' and ' + str(max_value) + ':')
    while int(guess) != x:
        if int(guess) < x:
            print('Too low!')
        elif int(guess) > x:
            print('Too high!')
        guess = input('Guess a number between ' + str(min_value) + ' and ' + str(max_value) + ':')
    print('Correct!')
In [123]:
guess_the_number(1,100)

Too low!

Too high!

Correct!

Tutorial 2 (23/01/2026) ¶

  • iPhone Pricing
    • Apple recently announced price reductions for its iPhone 16 models in Australia, citing local market conditions and exchange rate challenges. In the United States, however, the iPhone 16 Pro remains priced at $999, with a production cost of $568 per unit and monthly base sales of 200,000 units.
    • The executive team is considering whether a similar price adjustment in the US could increase profitability. Market data suggests that for every $1 reduction in price, demand increases by 1,000 units. However, the price must not fall below the production cost.
    • The team wants to identify the price point that maximises profits.
    • They anticipate that this pricing calculation will be valuable when evaluating strategies for other iPhone models, with different prices, production costs and base sales. Market data suggests that other iPhone models share the same price elasticity of demand.
  • Summary:
    • Apple iPhone 16 Pro priced at \$999, with production cost of \$568 per unit and monthly base sales of 200,000 units
    • For every \$1 reduction in price, demand increases by 1,000 units
    • We want to find the price that maximizes profits
In [124]:
def find_optimal_price(start_price, cost, base_sales):
    """ Find the optimal price of an iPhone model. """
    best_price, max_profit = 0, 0

    price = start_price
    while price > cost:
        # Calculate units sold accounting for price-demand
        units_sold = base_sales + (1_000 * (start_price - price))
        
        profit = (price - cost) * units_sold

        # Check if this is the highest profit so far
        if profit > max_profit:
            max_profit, best_price = profit, price

        price -= 1  # Try the next lower price

    return best_price, max_profit

Optimal price: $ 884
Maximum profit: $ 99540000
In [127]:
# iPhone Pro 16 parameters
start_price = 999
cost = 568
base_sales = 200_000

optimal_price, maximum_profit = find_optimal_price(start_price, cost, base_sales)
print("Optimal price: $", optimal_price)
print("Maximum profit: $", maximum_profit)

Optimal price: $ 884
Maximum profit: $ 99540000

Lecture 3: Lists and For Loops (27/01/2026) ¶

Live Class ¶

  • Goals:
    • Write for loops over range() and over sequences (lists/strings), including nested loops
    • Create and manipulate lists
    • Turn a problem statement into a clear algorithmic plan, then implement and test it on examples
    • Describe the core idea of binary search and why it needs about $log_2(n)$ guesses
  • A brain teaser:
    • You are trying to cook an egg for exactly 15 minutes. However, instead of a timer, you are given two ropes which burn for exactly 1 hour each. The ropes, however, are of uneven densities, i.e., half the rope lengthwise might take only two minutes to burn.
  • What do interviewers look for here? A systematic and logical approach
    • We know we can measure one hour by burning a rope.
    • Insight 1: we can also measure 30 mins, by burning a rope at both ends
    • Solution: we can also measure 15 mins, by lighting one rope at both ends and the other rope at one end. When the first rope is done, light the other end of the second one and start cooking your egg
      • When the second rope is done, your egg is cooked
In [ ]:
# i = 4
# while i > 2:
#     print(i*i)
# i = i-1
# Results in infinite loop
In [3]:
def fun_function(a,b):
    if b > a:
        return b
    print(a)
x = fun_function(3,4)
print(x)

4
In [128]:
def fun_function(a,b):
    if b > a:
        print(2*b)
    print(a)
x = fun_function(-3,-1) # None is assigned to x
print(abs(x))

-2
-3

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[128], line 6
      4     print(a)
      5 x = fun_function(-3,-1) # None is assigned to x
----> 6 print(abs(x))

TypeError: bad operand type for abs(): 'NoneType'
  • For loops loop for an item in a sequence
    • It goes through the elements in a sequence in order, executing the code block for each element until the sequence is exhausted
    • The keyword in is special and means that the for loop assigns the value in the sequence to element one by one, starting from the first value in the sequence
  • The general form is as follows:
 for item in sequence:
     statements
  • Range gives a collection of numbers in the form range(start, end, step)
    • This excludes the end number
    • range(n) gives integers from 0 to n-1
    • We can loop from high to low numbers by specifying a start higher than the end and a negative step size
In [7]:
for i in range(5, 11, 2):
    print(i)

5
7
9
In [8]:
for i in range(1,4):
    print(i)
    print(i*i)

1
1
2
4
3
9
In [50]:
for number in range(9,5,-1):
    print(number)

9
8
7
6
In [9]:
for i in range(1,4):
    print(i)
print('Hello')

1
2
3
Hello
In [10]:
s = 0
for i in range(3):
    for j in range(2):
        s += 1
print(s)

6
In [130]:
s = 'hiphop'
new_s = s[0:len(s):1] # same as range(), 0 is start, len(s) is end, and 1 is the step
new_s
Out[130]:
'hiphop'
In [21]:
new_s = s[::-1]
new_s
Out[21]:
'pohpih'
In [15]:
new_s = ''
for char in s:
    new_s = new_s + char
new_s
Out[15]:
'hiphop'
In [17]:
new_s = ''
for i in range(len(s)):
    new_s += s[len(s) - i - 1]
new_s
Out[17]:
'pohpih'
  • Lists are one way to represent ordered compound data
    • It has the syntax of square brackets and comma-separated values (which can be a mix of any Python objects)
In [22]:
names = [] # create empty list
names.append('Ruth') # add an item to the end of the list
names.append('Mo')
print(names)

['Ruth', 'Mo']
In [23]:
print(len(names))

2
In [24]:
print(names[0]) # indexing: 0, 1, 2, ..., we can also "slice": names[1:2]

Ruth
In [25]:
names [1] = 'Olivia' # change Mo -> Olivia
for name in names: # convenient for a for loop!
    print('Hello ' + name)

Hello Ruth
Hello Olivia
In [26]:
# we can also loop by accessing values at each index:
for index in range(len(names)):
    print('Hi ' + names[index] + ', your index is ' + str(index))

Hi Ruth, your index is 0
Hi Olivia, your index is 1
In [36]:
a = 'hip'
for i in range(1, len(a)):
    print(a[i] + a[i-1])

ih
pi
In [131]:
a = [5, 'hippo', 3]
b = [1]
# both are valid lists
In [42]:
s = 'hippopotamus'
print(s[0])
print(s[1:4])
print(s[4:9:2])
print(s[:len(s)-1])

h
ipp
ooa
hippopotamu
  • s[start:end:step] gets characters from start index to end -1 index with specified step size (which could be negative).
In [44]:
s1 = 'hiphop'
s2 = 'opotamus'
s = s1 + s2
print(s)
for char in s:
    print(char + '!')

hiphopopotamus
h!
i!
p!
h!
o!
p!
o!
p!
o!
t!
a!
m!
u!
s!
  • Methods are functions associated with a type of object, here strings
    • They are used with the “dot notation”
In [45]:
s = 'I like blackberries.'
new_s = s.replace('black', 'blue')
print(new_s)

I like blueberries.
  • Problem: Given a list containing numbers, write a function that returns the number of unique numbers in the list.
  • Search for Solution:
    • Make sure you understand the problem. Try example.
    • Design a solution. Use paper. Find patterns. Check module materials. Try things out. Solve a part of the problem or a special case.
    • Implement the solution. Start from small parts. If your code does not work, trace what the program does line by line to find where the problem is. Use the print function.
    • Test the solution with examples.
In [172]:
def count_unique_entries(input_list):
    unique_list = []
    
    for entry in input_list:
        if entry not in unique_list:
            unique_list.append(entry)
            
    unique_count = len(unique_list)

    return unique_count


list1 = [3, 5, 6, 2, 6, 7, 8, 2]

print(len(list1))
print(count_unique_entries(list1))

8
6
  • Better approach would be to sort the list first
    • After sorting, equal items are adjacent, so you can count uniques in one pass
  • Problem: Given two strings, write a method to decide whether one string is a permutation of the other. Use the same strategies as the last problem.
  • Solutions
    • Obvious check: are strings of same length?
    • Sort both strings, and see if identical when sorted
    • Alternatively, we know there are 26 letters in the alphabet
      • Define array with 26 entries all equal to 0
      • When character in first string is in ith position, increase ith entry by 1
      • When character in second string is in ith position, decrease ith entry by 1
      • Strings match if and only if the final array is all zeroes
In [141]:
def permute(string_1, string_2):
    count = [0] * 26
    alphabet = 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz'
    for char in string_1:
        for index in range(len(alphabet)):
            if alphabet[index] == char:
                count[index] += 1
    for char in string_2:
        for index in range(len(alphabet)):
            if alphabet[index] == char:
                count[index] -= 1
    if count == [0]*26:
        return True
    else:
        return False
In [146]:
permute('declamations', 'anecdotalism')
Out[146]:
True
In [147]:
permute('isthistrue', 'noitisnt')
Out[147]:
False
  • Problem: Write a function that given an input $n$, outputs the first $n$ Fibonacci numbers
  • Solutions:
    • Handle base cases where $n=1$ and $n=2$ separately
    • Use two integer variables, one that tracks current number, one that tracks prev number, and iteratively print and update
In [49]:
def print_fibonacci(n):
    a = 1 # current value
    b = 0 # previous value
    for i in range(n):
        print(a)
        c = a
        a = a + b
        b = c

print_fibonacci(5)

1
1
2
3
5
  • Case Study: we'd like to design an algorithm that guesses a number between 1 and 100 as quickly as possible
    • The user guesses a number, and doesn't tell the program
    • The program makes a guess, adn the user says 'Higher', 'Lower', or 'Correct'
    • The program stops running when it correctly guesses the number
  • Incorrect version:
def guess_the_number_game():
    print("Think of a number between 1 and 100.")
    lower_limit, upper_limit = 1, 100
    attempts = 0

while True:
    # Make a guess
    computer_guess = int(lower_limit + upper_limit / 2)
    print(f"My guess is: {computer_guess}")

    user_feedback = input("Is your number 'Higher', 'Lower', or 'Correct'? ")

    # Update the guess range based on user feedback
    if user_feedback == 'higher':
        lower_limit = computer_guess + 1
    elif user_feedback == 'lower':
        upper_limit = computer_guess - 1
    else:
        print(f"Yay! I guessed your number {computer_guess} in {attempts} attempts.")
        break

    attempts += 1

guess_the_number_game()
  • Corrected version:
In [148]:
def guess_the_number_game():
    print("Think of a number between 1 and 100.")
    lower_limit, upper_limit = 1, 100
    attempts = 0

    while True:
        # Make a guess
        computer_guess = int((lower_limit + upper_limit) / 2) # add brackets so division applies to both lower and upper
        print(f"My guess is: {computer_guess}")
        attempts += 1 # corrected position tobefore the input

        user_feedback = input("Is your number 'Higher', 'Lower', or 'Correct'? ")

        # Update the guess range based on user feedback
        if user_feedback == 'Higher': # corrected 'higher' to 'Higher'
            lower_limit = computer_guess + 1
        elif user_feedback == 'Lower': # corrected 'lower' to 'Lower'
            upper_limit = computer_guess - 1
        elif user_feedback == 'Correct': # corrected else statement to elif
            print(f"Yay! I guessed your number {computer_guess} in {attempts} attempts.")
            break
        else:
            print(f"Error")
            break
        
guess_the_number_game()

Think of a number between 1 and 100.
My guess is: 50

My guess is: 25

My guess is: 12

My guess is: 18

My guess is: 15

My guess is: 13

Yay! I guessed your number 13 in 6 attempts.
  • The binary search menthod terminates after $log_2(n)$ iterations, where $n$ is the size of the gap between the upper and lower limits
    • Each time we make a guess, we partition the space of numbers in 2
    • After $k$ iterations, we could be in any one of $2^k$ partitions
    • When $2^k \ge n$, we are done, so $$ min_{k \in \mathbb{N}} k : 2^k \ge n \Longrightarrow k \approx log_2(n) $$
  • For loops are often more convenient than while loops when we are working with specific sequences, and we know how many times we need to iterate
    • While loops can be very useful in applications where we don't know how many iterations our calculations will require

Python Exercises ¶

  • Readings:
    • Guttag Chapters 2.6, 5.1-5.5
    • OR
    • Sweigart, Al. Automate the Boring Stuff with Python.
      • Chapter 2 – Control flow https://automatetheboringstuff.com/chapter2/
      • Chapter 4 – Lists https://automatetheboringstuff.com/chapter4/

Question 1: Recap ¶

  1. n = 3
while n >= 1:
    n = n - 1
    print(n)
    1. (line 1) 2
    2. (line 2) 1
    3. (line 3) 0
  2. n = 0.8
while n <= 1:
    n = n * n
    print(n)
    1. Infinite loop
  3. def some_function(i):
    if type(i) == str:
        print('A string')
    else:
        print('Something else')
some_function('Hello there!' + str(float('3.14')))
    1. A string

For loops and range() ¶

  • Previously used while loops as such:
In [149]:
number = 0
while number <= 4:
    print(number*number)
    number += 1

0
1
4
9
16
  • Same solution using for loop:
In [150]:
for number in range(5):
    print(number*number)

0
1
4
9
16
  • General structure of a for loop:
for element in sequence:
    # statements
  • For loop goes through the elements in a sequence in order, executing the code block for each element until the sequence is exhausted
  • One by one, starting from the first value in the sequence, the for loop assigns the value to the variable element, after which the code block is executed
    • The code above assigns values 0 to 4 to number
  • range reates a structure housing a sequence of integer values based on its input
In [151]:
for number in range(1, 3):
    print(number)

1
2
In [152]:
for number in range(3, 8, 2):
    print(number)

3
5
7
In [153]:
for number in range(9, 5, -1):
    print(number)

9
8
7
6
In [154]:
for i in range(3):
    print('Outer loop, for value i=' + str(i))
    for j in range(2):
        print('Inner loop, for value j=' + str(j))

Outer loop, for value i=0
Inner loop, for value j=0
Inner loop, for value j=1
Outer loop, for value i=1
Inner loop, for value j=0
Inner loop, for value j=1
Outer loop, for value i=2
Inner loop, for value j=0
Inner loop, for value j=1
  • break statement stops a loop before the sequence is finished
In [155]:
a = 10
for i in range(a):
    print(i)
    if i == 2:
        break

0
1
2
  • For loops are often more convenient than while loops when we are working with specific sequences, and we know how many times we need to iterate
  • While loops can be very useful in applications where we don't know how many iterations our calculation will require

Question 2: Loops ¶

  1. temp = 0
for i in range(5):
    temp += 2
print(temp)
    1. 10
  2. cnt1 = 0
cnt2 = 0
for i in range(2):
    cnt1 += 1
    for j in range(3):
        cnt2 += 1
print(cnt1, cnt2)
    1. 2 6
  3. def loop_fun(n):
    x = n
    for i in range(n):
        y = 0
        for j in range(n):
            y += x
        x += y
    return x
loop_fun(2)
    1. 18

Lists ¶

  • List: ordered collection of items
    • Defined using square brackets and the items within the list are separated by a comma
In [158]:
L = [1,2,3]
type(L)
Out[158]:
list
In [159]:
number_list = [1, 2, 3]
word_list = ['this', 'is', 'a', 'list']
a = 5
b = 'good'
mixed_list = [a, b]
In [161]:
prices = [400, 450, 465, 525, 650, 700]

prices[0]
Out[161]:
400
In [162]:
prices[1:3]
Out[162]:
[450, 465]
In [163]:
prices[-1]
Out[163]:
700
In [164]:
prices[:-1]
Out[164]:
[400, 450, 465, 525, 650]
In [165]:
prices[::2] # every other element
Out[165]:
[400, 465, 650]
  • We can access a "slice" of the list prices using the syntax prices[begin:end:step]
    • Slice will include the first begin-th element but exclude the final end-th element (just like range)
  • We can change elements of list and create new lists from existing ones
In [29]:
prices[0] = 405 # assigns 405 to the 0th index of prices list

prices.append(675) # append adds an item to the end

print(prices)

[405, 450, 465, 525, 650, 700, 675]
In [166]:
new_prices = [750, 790, 890, 975, 1195, 1200]

all_prices = prices + new_prices # add new_prices to the end of prices, create new result list
prices.extend(new_prices) # append all items of new_prices to prices 

print(all_prices)

print(len(prices))

[400, 450, 465, 525, 650, 700, 750, 790, 890, 975, 1195, 1200]
12

Looping through lists ¶

  • You can loop through the elements of a list
In [31]:
for price in prices:
    print('The price was ' + str(price))

The price was 405
The price was 450
The price was 465
The price was 525
The price was 650
The price was 700
The price was 675
The price was 750
The price was 790
The price was 890
The price was 975
The price was 1195
The price was 1200
  • When we want indices, we take range(len(list))
In [32]:
# We use range on the length of prices to make sure we go through all list indices.
for index in range(len(prices)):
    price = prices[index]
    print('The price was ' + str(price))

The price was 405
The price was 450
The price was 465
The price was 525
The price was 650
The price was 700
The price was 675
The price was 750
The price was 790
The price was 890
The price was 975
The price was 1195
The price was 1200
  • When comparing current index element with the next index element, we look only through range(len(list)-1) to avoid calling a nonexistent index
In [34]:
for index in range(len(prices) - 1):
    if prices[index + 1] > prices[index]:
        print('Prices went up at index ' + str(index))
    else: print('Prices went down at index ' + str(index))

Prices went up at index 0
Prices went up at index 1
Prices went up at index 2
Prices went up at index 3
Prices went up at index 4
Prices went down at index 5
Prices went up at index 6
Prices went up at index 7
Prices went up at index 8
Prices went up at index 9
Prices went up at index 10
Prices went up at index 11
  • Looping through values is easier when all we need to do is something based on each value
    • Loop with comparison is easier with indices

Lists within lists ¶

  • Python lists are flexible and can contain different types of data, other lists, etc.
    • Nested List: list that contains another list
    • We reference elements within nested lists by first calling the nested list index and the index of the element within the nested list
In [167]:
L = [1, 2, 4, 5, 'a', 5, [2, 3]]
In [35]:
visited_cities = [['Paris', 'France'], ['Berlin', 'Germany'], ['Bucharest', 'Romania'], ['Reykjavik', 'Iceland'], ['Munich', 'Germany']]

visited_cities[1][0]
Out[35]:
'Berlin'
In [36]:
german_cities = [] # creates empty list
for pair in visited_cities:
    if pair[1] == 'Germany':
        german_cities.append(pair[0])
print(german_cities) 

['Berlin', 'Munich']

Methods ¶

  • Methods: actions associated with a data type
    • Most methods are accessed using the dot notation, like L.extend([1,4])
Method Description
L = [1,2,3,4,5,'Joe'] create a list containg the items inside the brackets
L = list(range(0,6)) create a list containg a sequence of variables
len(L) returns the number of items in the list
L.append('Mike') add an item ('Mike') at the end of the list
L.remove('Joe') remove the first item in the list with this value ('Joe')
L.pop(4) remove the item of the list with this index (4)
L.insert(3,'Kate') insert the item with this value ('Kate') in this position (3)
L.extend([1,4]) add items from an iterable e.g list ([1,4]) at the end of the list
L.sort() sorts the list
L.reverse() reverses the list
In [38]:
L = [1]

L.append([1,4])

print(L)

[1, [1, 4]]
In [39]:
L = [1]

L.extend([1,4])

print(L)

[1, 1, 4]
  • In Python, copying a list using = does not create a new copy of the list data in the computer's memory
    • We instead copy the reference from the name list1 to data, not the actual data
    • Names list1 and list2 both point to the same list in memory
    • To create a new list, we slice the list or use the built-in function list()
In [169]:
list1 = [1,2,3]
list2 = list1
list1.append(5)
print(list2)

[1, 2, 3, 5]
In [176]:
list1 = [1,2,3]
list2 = list1[:] # slice that gets all items
list3 = list(list1) # function that creates a new list of the items
list1.append(5)

print(list2)
print(list3)

[1, 2, 3]
[1, 2, 3]

Question 3: Lists ¶

  1. l1 = [17, 'a', 2, 'tokyo']
len(l1)
    1. 4
  2. l1 = [17, 'a', 2, 'tokyo']
l2 = ['g', 3, 'k']
len(l1) + len(l2)
    1. 7
  3. l1 = [17, 'a', 2, 'tokyo']
l1[2]
    1. 2
  4. l1 = [17, 'a', 2, 'tokyo']
l1[1:3]
    1. ['a', 2]
  5. l1 = [1, 5, 3]
for number in l1:
    print(number**2)
    1. (line 1) 1
    2. (line 2) 25
    3. (line 3) 9
  6. visited_cities = [['Paris', 'France'], ['Berlin', 'Germany'], ['Bucharest', 'Romania']]
print(visited_cities[0])
    1. ['Paris', 'France']
  7. visited_cities = [['Paris', 'France'], ['Berlin', 'Germany'], ['Bucharest', 'Romania']]
print(visited_cities[2][1])
    1. Romania
  8. visited_cities = [['Paris', 'France'], ['Berlin', 'Germany'], ['Bucharest', 'Romania']]
print(visited_cities[2][2])
    1. Error
  9. visited_cities = [['Paris', 'France'], ['Berlin', 'Germany'], ['Bucharest', 'Romania']]
for pair in visited_cities:
    print(pair[1])
    1. (line 1) France
    2. (line 2) Germany
    3. (line 3) Romania
  10. # country and its population in millions
countries = [['France', 65], ['Germany', 80], ['Italy', 60]]
pop_sum = 0
for item in countries:
    pop_sum += item[1]
    print(pop_sum)
    1. (line 1) 65
    2. (line 2) 145
    3. (line 3) 205
  11. countries = [['France', 65], ['Germany', 80], ['Italy', 60]]
for item in countries:
    item[1] += 1
print(countries[1][1])
    1. 81
  12. countries = [['France', 65], ['Germany', 80], ['Italy', 60]]
populations = []
for item in countries:
    item[1] += 1
    populations.append(item[1])
print(populations)
    1. [66, 81, 61]

Working with text ¶

  • Manipulating, parsing, or otherwise working with text is very common task
  • Strings are like lists in that you can go access and go through particular elements (characters)

Slicing and combining strings ¶

  • We can slice strings by index just like lists, with the character of the string as our "elements"
In [51]:
test_word = 'lovely'

# String slicing
print(test_word[1:5])
print(test_word[:-1])
print(test_word[5:1:-2])
print(test_word[::-1])

ovel
lovel
ye
ylevol
  • We can "add" strings together using the + sign
In [52]:
print(test_word + ' ' + test_word[:2])

lovely lo

Looping through strings ¶

  • We can loop through a string just like a list using a for loop and the operator in
In [53]:
vowels = 'aeiouy'

for letter in vowels:
    print(letter) 

a
e
i
o
u
y

Immutable strings ¶

  • Even though strings are very similar to lists, they are immutable
    • Immutable: cannot change
In [55]:
a_list = [1, 5, 9]
a_list[1] = 3 # OK with a list
print(a_list) 

a_string = 'fire'
a_string[1] = 'a' # not OK with a string
print(a_string)

[1, 3, 9]

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[55], line 6
      3 print(a_list) 
      5 a_string = 'fire'
----> 6 a_string[1] = 'a' # not OK with a string
      7 print(a_string)

TypeError: 'str' object does not support item assignment

String methods ¶

  • Like with lists, there exist many built-in methods that work specifically for strings
Method Description
s = 'lovely weather' Create string
s.capitalize() Capitalizes first letter of s
s.lower() Converts all uppercase letters in s to lowercase
s.upper() Converts all lowercase letters in s to uppercase)
s.startswith('love') Checks if s starts with specified string
len(s) Returns the length of the s
s.find('v') Finds the first index at which v occurs in s
s.replace(old, new) Replaces all occurrences of old in s with new
  • Sometimes we want to split strings into parts (e.g. sentence into words)
  • We can use the split method that breaks a string into a list
    • Default is a space, but we can also specify the delimeter
In [57]:
sentence = 'The Python language was created by Guido van Rossum. It was named after Monty Python.'
sent_list = sentence.split() # default is split by space
print(sent_list)
sent_list = sentence.split('.') # split by full stop
print(sent_list)

['The', 'Python', 'language', 'was', 'created', 'by', 'Guido', 'van', 'Rossum.', 'It', 'was', 'named', 'after', 'Monty', 'Python.']
['The Python language was created by Guido van Rossum', ' It was named after Monty Python', '']
  • We can also do the opposite, joining together list values into a single string
In [181]:
german_cities = ['Berlin', 'Munich', 'Frankfurt'] 

print(', '.join(german_cities))

Berlin, Munich, Frankfurt
  • The string.format() method is useful for displaying values and variables
In [59]:
x = 3
print('The numbers are {0} and {1}.'.format(x, 4.2)) # 0 and 1 are indices for input of format()

The numbers are 3 and 4.2.
  • We can combine join and format
In [60]:
print('We visited {0} during the trip.'.format(', '.join(german_cities)))

We visited Berlin, Munich, Frankfurt during the trip.
  • We can use f-strings from Python v 3.6 onwards to do the same thing, inputting variables directly
In [183]:
print(f'We visited {german_cities[0]} first.')

We visited Berlin first.
  • There are so many built-in methods that you can't remember them all
    • Search Engine, Stack Overflow, and Python's help is good place to start
In [62]:
help(str.split) # help on specific method
# help(str) # help on the str data type - lists all methods

Help on method_descriptor:

split(self, /, sep=None, maxsplit=-1) unbound builtins.str method
    Return a list of the substrings in the string, using sep as the separator string.

      sep
        The separator used to split the string.

        When set to None (the default value), will split on any whitespace
        character (including \n \r \t \f and spaces) and will discard
        empty strings from the result.
      maxsplit
        Maximum number of splits.
        -1 (the default value) means no limit.

    Splitting starts at the front of the string and works to the end.

    Note, str.split() is mainly useful for data that has been intentionally
    delimited.  With natural text that includes punctuation, consider using
    the regular expression module.

Question 4: More on Strings ¶

  1. word = 'hello!'
print(word[1:3])
    1. el
  2. word = 'hello!'
len(word)
    1. 6
  3. word = 'hElLO!'
print((word.lower()).capitalize())
    1. Hello!

Question 5: Looping practice ¶

  1. Implement the function item_lengths
In [63]:
def item_lengths(input_list):
    """
